speaker: interviewer
text: Today is May 8, 1995. The survivor is Clara Dreier-- D-R-E-I-E-R. The interviewer is Ruth Resnikoff-- R-E-S-N-I-K-O-F-F. The city is Coconut Creek in the Miami, Florida, USA District. The language will be English and this is role number one.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to introduce Clara Dreier. We are in her home in Coconut Creek, Florida. And I am Ruth Resnikoff-- R-E-S-N-I-K-O-F-F. And today is May 9, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to introduce my husband, Sal Dreier. We are married 37 years. We have four children and four grandchildren. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us your full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Clara Dreier.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clara Brill.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your date of birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brody, in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the population of Brody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Population was maybe 80,000. With Jews, we have like 12 and a half thousand Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother died very early. My grandfather I remember a little bit. He was in business. Then the other set of grandparents from my father's side, during the German occupation, they moved in to Brody. But it had been too long, so I really didn't know them too well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go to visit them as a little child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once in a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got talking. Not really long-- I was there for a few hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father died very early-- 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was wrong?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a heart attack. He was only 30-some years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were about 10 when he died?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 10 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't have many memories of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember, but not many.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was killed during the German occupation. She was one of the first person taken away. And soon the German war came, you know, to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before that, can--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before that, she was in business, because we had no father. She had to support four children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She took over my father's business and my grandfather left her a business. My grandfather left the grocery store. My father had beer business too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was working with both?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you help?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once and a while I left the school, I walked in and I helped. My older sister helped a little, but they were still in school. Still, they used to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- who is in the home to cook?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a maid. Her name, you want to have, was Stephanie, so she used to take care of the house and the kids, take to school, bring from school. And then we had people coming-- one used to wash clothes and one helped, because my mother was always so busy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any memories from your pre-school time? Just a little child, things you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my sisters. And I remember when my brother was born. I was only five years old. He was the first boy in the family. It was a big, big, you know, occasion. We-- one sister was very close. She was still older than I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Hilda ?] And we used to go out together to friends. We used to enjoy very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: When that boy was born, did you feel-- do you remember feeling that you weren't important anymore? Or something--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were all, you know, very close family. But my mother always wants to have a boy. And-- and he's here today.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's still living.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful. Did you live in a house or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We lived in an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like? How big? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first, we lived in my father-- live much bigger apartment. Then he died, we moved closer to the business. So we had like two bedrooms, a kitchen, and like a dining area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have running water in the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No? Where was the water?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to go down the street. The maid used to bring over fresh water.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about electricity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Electricity we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a refrigerator?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Radio we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do before the war, for pleasures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war for pleasure, I used to go to school. I used to study. I had at lot of friends. I used to go out to the movies.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of movies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shirley Temple, I remember. [INAUDIBLE], I remember a movie we watched. Charlie Chaplin still did. And we used to go skating sometimes, used to go to the parks. We had all kind-- swimming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever take vacation?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to go every summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the country with the maid. My mother used to come and visit us during the weekend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you stay with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the maid.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the maid's home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We used to rent a place, like a bungalow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. I see. So your mother left you there with the maid? She must have been a very reliable person.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was reliable. It was very close-- not far along [? to ?] [? go to. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about the Jewish life that you led-- Shabbat, holidays--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father died early. When he was alive, we used to have every holidays, like Passover, Seder, you know. When he died, it was a little bit different. But we knew exactly-- Friday nights, she has to come all the way home, put the candle lights, and have dinner.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat for dinner on--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dinner like a real Jewish, you know-- gefilte fish, chicken soup, and all kinds of meats, fruits. And-- and Saturday, we had a kosher home. Saturday was a big meal we all enjoyed together. We used to go to picnics with the family. It was really nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- do you remember any holiday rituals? Things you would do on Rosh Hashanah? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosh Hashanah usually we used to go to the shul like in the morning till dinnertime with my mother, get dressed, and come have dinner. And it was a holiday. We couldn't do too much because everything was closed. So we spent with friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in a Jewish area? With everyone in the apartment building Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly was Jews-- was mixed, but mostly where I used to live in this building, was Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any anti-Semitism between the tenants?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was too young to think about-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your schooling.

speaker: interviewee
text: The schooling-- I used to go to a private school. It was [INAUDIBLE]. It means Jewish educational center. And I used to take Hebrew, Polish, Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh you learned Hebrew in that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And after school, I used to go special to Hebrew school. I used to go Bais Yaakov. And except this, my mother hired home-- a rabbi used to come and teach us. We never want to take Hebrew. So the rabbi used to come. We used to hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother? You?

speaker: interviewee
text: I and my brother, everyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Used to hide? [LAUGHING] Did they find you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, in the house. Then after school, you know, school friends, we used to go and enjoy. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in school, as it was all Jewish, there was no anti-Semitism, so did--

speaker: interviewee
text: School where I went-- all Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: As you walked home through the streets, there was no name-calling?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was too young.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the 1930s when Hitler came to power in Germany, you had a radio in your home. Did you listen to his speeches?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Were you aware of the restrictions that were-- the laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a lot of people came from Germany to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you understand why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I understood. So the people came. They had families. They came with kids. So I understood why. But we didn't know what's happening at that time. As children, I mean-- probably my mother and family--

speaker: interviewer
text: No. I suppose, as your father wasn't there, there was no one that she could discuss it with. I mean, many-- many people tell me, yes, I heard my parents discuss it, but your mother was alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We heard. We heard from parents, my mother, with-- my mother's friends used to come to the house. They used to discuss-- of course we heard. Then my neighbor had even a family from Germany, staying with them. So we heard a lot. But you know, we were kids. We didn't pay that much attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the day the war began?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the day the war began-- 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, the Russian war came in 1939 till '41. We had the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Tell me the historical-- the-- the Russians came into Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took over Brody?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took over Brody. And the life was just normal. People-- I mean, my mother was still in business. I was going back to school. So we didn't know much, you know, difference. Then later, they started taking away people's homes, everything belonged to them, like we had to move from our apartment to another.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians did this.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a Communist action, or was it against Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Communist action. I didn't feel, at that time, nothing against Jews. Maybe I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you move to another apartment. Was it as a good or not as good?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was much smaller. And we lived with another lady. She owned the place. She took us in, till the German came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was no shortage of food. Or was there? Was there a shortage of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not during the Germans, especially we-- my mother was in business, so she always had the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did the Germans come?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans came in 1942. It was in July.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then what happened, one Saturday morning, I went out with my older sister, my brother, to friends to visit. When we came back, my mother and the oldest sister was gone. The people told me the gestapo came. They took her. She was actually the first one taken away during the German's occupation. And they came with a picture of my second sister-- this one, she went with me out. And they said, if she's not gonna or the mother stays, they're gonna kill the mother and the older sister. So she didn't ask nobody questions. She went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea why they picked your-- your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: The youngest one with the picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because her picture was always in the main street in the studio. She was very pretty.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took out the picture from the window and they came-- they figure maybe she's A communist, maybe she's something to do with the Russians. Who knows. And my mother? I don't know-- just probably somebody show her the finger, the maid, or the-- a lot of Polacks-- you never know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me about the maid.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there was-- when they took out the [INAUDIBLE] the neighbors, I wasn't there. Because when they took my mother-- so one maid was staying downstairs, and laughing. So probably somebody just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to your mother? Did you ever find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: They kept her with my sister for a while in a school-- a business school we had. And later, they start taking old professional people-- doctors, lawyers, teachers-- and all of them got killed. One day, I heard screams in the street. I was living with my aunt at that time, because I was left with my brother alone. And I heard screams. I go out to the front of the door. And I saw two trucks with people screaming, and I saw my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my two sisters, screaming. And I start running after the people-- after the trucks. So [INAUDIBLE] stopped with the machine guns going to shoot me. So I went back to my aunt's house. And I never saw them again. They took them to the woods, and they all got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: This aunt you were with-- was it your mother's sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Father's sister. So you stayed with her, now with your one little brother-- no with your younger sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: With my younger brother, I stayed--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, brother, sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to the end, till the ghetto was liquidated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when did they make a ghetto in Brody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ghetto was in 1942 in December.

speaker: interviewer
text: This house with your aunt, was that in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We had to move. In January everybody's supposed to be in the ghettos. We were living with my aunt. She had seven her children. And she had one adopted daughter, married with a child, and her oldest daughter with a child. So we were like 15 people in two rooms-- one room with a little kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto. Before the ghetto, she had a much, little bigger apartment, but it was still [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was the ghetto roped off? Was there a wall? Was there barbed--

speaker: interviewee
text: Wires.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wire. Did they have a curfew, that you could go and come in the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: We could go out and come wherever we wanted. We had a Jewish police too, which they were watching. We had a Judenrat. But before the ghetto, even we used to live during the Germans, we had two-- twice, they made like a pogroms. Do you know what's a pogrom?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. But tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to take people and kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the ghetto, did the conditions of food and-- and heating become worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: It became less. It was not so easy. But somehow we managed. Some people didn't have-- they were hungry. They were dying in the streets, swollen. Typhus-- a lot of people were sick. I was one of the lucky ones. I didn't get the typhus. And it was not easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have any opportunity to get away? To run into the country yet? Did you have any jewelry, or things that you could use to help yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had-- my mother left was everything hidden under the ground. So one day we were sitting in a bunker. My cousins were very handy. And under the cellar, we had bunkers. We used to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this-- in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the ghetto and in the ghetto. We had the same thing. This happened in the ghetto. We were sitting under the cellar, there was like a little-- not a cellar, but [? water full. ?] So my sister was ready to get married before they killed her. So the whole fiance, he knew everything that my mother had. So he took me one evening out from the-- that basement, and he showed me where everything lays. And he even helped me to dig out everything from under the ground. So I had a little money and jewelry that I kept with me, which helped me for some time not too long.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you use it? How did it help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: First there was liquidation ghetto. We were laying in a bunker, with uncle, with the aunt, with the kids, and a lot of neighbors. So how it happened, they opened in the-- in the city, they opened a little labor camp. Whoever was left over, they found people to open the labor camp. And they used the people to work on them. So one fellow, he knew me. He took me out from that bunker. He brought me to that camp. In that camp, you have to pay-- everything. I had a lot of Russian gold money.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had one cousin, which I liked very much. I didn't want to leave her in the bunker. My brother was too young to take in to labor camp. They would kill him anyways. At the same time, he had typhus in the bunker. There was-- I was lucky. I was laying with all the people that were sick and I didn't get sick. So I paid for her to [INAUDIBLE] Russian, they're called 20 rubles. You had to pay to get into the work camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: People, they used to make, like on the side-- you know. So I went to that labor camp, and I took my cousin. She got sick. She had to do with her lung-- she was spitting up blood. So when I had been in that labor camp, the same guy, took me out. He said, you know what? For you, it's the best thing to go out every day to work. Don't stay in that camp, because one day they're gonna liquidate that camp too. I said, that's fine. I go out. There was one of the policeman, Jewish-- he used to take me every day out and bring me to a place that was like an estate I used to call [INAUDIBLE] and used to live a princess there. And then they used to bring all the furniture from the Jews, from the ghettos, all the clothing, silver, everything. So he put me there to work, to select clothing, to, you know just for be occupied not to stay in that camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I've been there. And one day, the guy comes and says to me-- we had one little room. We got together more, a couple people. We put furniture against the wall, that you wouldn't see it's a room. Daytime we used to open, and go out to work. And nights, we used to lock ourself in. Nobody should find us. One day comes a guy-- he used to be like guard outside, watching the estate. He comes to us and says, you know what? Whoever has any money, give it to me. Let's hide some place because some partisans are coming from the woods, and they know you people are here. They're gonna you. And I didn't know the guy. I didn't trust him. So I said-- I didn't say anything when he left the room. I had a mattress. I put everything under the mattress. In the evening, what happened-- two, three guys come with masks, with guns, and opened the door. They knew exactly how to open it, to move the furniture. Somebody probably squealed something. And he comes to me first. He said, where's your money? I said, I have no money. The Russian language he said to me-- first of all, he walks in, he says, hands up. Who was he? A Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Jew. They were partisans. They were living in the woods, hiding just the same like we were hiding. And I say, I have no money. And another woman had money. And he starts checking the place. And he found everything under the mattress. What could you find in one little room? He found, he took away. I had that little coat still, from home. He took me on his side. He said in the Russian, that blonde one, we're gonna take with us. So I start crying. I said take anything he wanted but just leave me here. I didn't know who they were. But later I find out. So I had that coat. He took the coat from me. He had a girlfriend at home in the woods. So he needed that coat for her. I was left even without a coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a Jew stealing from Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what? I recognized him in [? Austria. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And later, we were working. One day, one guy-- he knew me very good-- he comes to me and says, you know what? They're liquidating the labor camp. I don't want you to go back. You stay here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, what's gonna happen? Why are you going back? He was eating strawberries. He said, I am the police. They're gonna let me go. They won't take me. He never came back. And they brought the clothing from the people. I recognized his jacket. And I knew he had in that jacket under the lining a lot of money saved. But I couldn't go down and take out-- you know. How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was very close with him. We were talking. He said, I have everything sewn in my lining in the jacket in case I'm gonna need it in the future to hide or-- So at the same time came the four SS men from the camp. And whoever was working that estate got us together, and they want to take us back to the camp. So we were marching, like six or eight people, and four gestapo on four corners. And the one was near me. He said, with the shotguns, with his-- I don't remember if it was a gun or machine gun-- he said to me in German, go. So I start running. And I start hiding in that estate. Then hours later, I start coming out, and I don't see anybody. But slowly, slowly, we met a few people. We got together, and we stayed in that place already. At the same time, we met a-- he was not German, he was a Volksdeutsche. He was Polack but he was working for the Germans. During the ghettos and during the Germans, he used to help to kill the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: But later, he knew the end was so close. He wants to save his life. So he wants to help us. He says, I'm very close with the princess. What about we're going to take one room in the top. We're going to close up and you people stay there. We're going to bring you food twice a day. Whatever. We said fine, very good. We didn't trust him but still, what can you lose? So the closed the last room in the top. And once in a while, she used to make us Polish sausages, something-- The princess made it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he used to deliver us twice-- we lived in one room. The windows was closed. The-- papers, anything they shouldn't know even anybody's there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for toilets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside, in the room, in a little pot. Couldn't help. You were-- we were maybe ten people. And he was sometimes there. Once he comes to us, he says, I can't keep you any longer. I don't know. He was leaving, or the princess threw him out, or something. Maybe she left. You have to leave the place everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I tell you what to do, to me he says. The rest of the people, I don't know where they went. I want you go back to the labor camp, where you used to be. You go upstairs in the attic. And we're going to keep you there and some more people that I met. And I'm gonna arrange-- they're going to take you to countries out of town. And you're going to be having some Polacks. They're going to save you. They're going to keep you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everything was for money. I didn't have already the money they took away, but I had other people. The want us to help the other. I'm laying in that attic. I know I left my brother, still in the ghetto, in that bunker with typhus. And I said to one guy that was with us, I can't leave him there. He said, child, he's probably-- if he's alive-- so let's take him out. He went, he took him out during the night. He brought him over. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Remember, because this is so interesting, but we've got to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [INAUDIBLE].
